ASSESSMENT GUIDE FINS3646 Toolkit for Finance Term 1, 2026

Assessment Summary Assessment Task % Due Date* Learning Outcomes Assessment 1: Weekly Quizzes (Individual) 15% Weeks 1-10 CLO2 The weekly online quizzes provide you with opportunities to apply the content discussed during the videos, lecture and coding demonstrations. Assessment 2: Project 1 (Individual) 20% Week 5 CLO1, CLO2 In this project, you will creatively combine and apply concepts developed over the first half of the term to solve problems related to the collection, cleaning, and/or analysis of real-world financial data. Assessment 3: Project 2 (Group) 30% Week 10 CLO1, CLO2, CLO3, CLO4 In groups, you will combine and apply concepts developed over the entire term to solve complex problems related to the collection, cleaning, and/or analysis of real-world financial data. You will work as a team, allocating tasks and responsibilities across members, using tools to promote codevelopment of solutions. Final Exam (Individual) 35% Exam CLO2, CLO3 Period You will be assessed on your understanding of this course and knowledge of programming in solving finance problems. * Due dates are set at Australian Eastern Standard/Daylight Time (AEST/AEDT). If you are in a different time zone, you can use the time and date converter . Course Learning Outcomes (CLO) CLO1: Develop a toolkit of essential financial market tools applicable to various real-world scenarios. [PLO 1, 2] CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data. [PLO1, 2] CLO3: Design tools to test predictions derived from financial theory using real world data. [PLO 1, 2] CLO4: Collaborate in technical financial team projects, communicating processes and findings to diverse audiences. [PLO 3, 4]

Assessment Policies Icon legend Due Date Weighting Format Length/Duration Submission Late Submissions The parameters for late submissions are outlined in the UNSW Assessment Implementation Procedure . For FINS3646, if you submit your assessments after the due date, you will incur penalties for late submission unless you have Special Consideration (see below). Late submission incurs a 5% penalty per day (including weekends), calculated from the marks allocated to that assessment (not your grade). Assessments will not be accepted more than 5 days late. Extensions You are expected to manage your time to meet assessment due dates. If you do require an extension to your assessment, please make a request as early as possible before the due date via the special consideration portal on myUNSW (My Student profile > Special Consideration). You can find more information on Special Consideration and the application process below. Lecturers and tutors do not have the ability to grant extensions. Special Consideration Special consideration is the process for assessing the impact of short-term events beyond your control (exceptional circumstances), on your performance in a specific assessment task. What are circumstances beyond my control? These are exceptional circumstances or situations that may: • Prevent you from completing a course requirement, • Keep you from attending an assessment, • Stop you from submitting an assessment, • Significantly affect your assessment performance. Available here is a list of circumstances that may be beyond your control. This is only a list of examples, and your exact circumstances may not be listed. You can find more detail and the application form on the Special Consideration site, or in the UNSW Special Consideration Application and Assessment Information for Students . Referencing • Refer to the student support guide on referencing for assignments: use this link .

Assessment 1: Weekly Quizzes Weeks 1-10 15% Online Weekly Quizzes, submitted on Sundays, 11:59pm Ed platform CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data. Task Description The weekly activities provide opportunities for you to practice the content discussed in each class. There will be a weekly quiz containing a mix of multiple-choice questions, short answer questions, and code challenges. Instructions • You need to complete all quizzes to receive full credit. • You can submit multiple times before the due date without penalty. Only your last submission will be marked. • Late submissions will be penalised by 5% per day, capped at five days starting immediately after the deadline. After 5 days, submissions will not be accepted and will be recorded as a zero. For example, if you submit your answers 1 minute after the deadline but within 24 hours after, we will deduct 5% from your marks for that week. • Please see the Quizzes slide on the Ed platform for more information. Submission • Via the Ed platform Grading and Feedback This assessment consists of: • 8 Weekly Quizzes – 15% evenly allocated across 8 quizzes. o Individual questions and code challenges are categorised into slides on the Ed platform. o Each slide is worth an equal percentage out of 100% o Individual questions or code challenges within a slide are weighted in proportion to the listed point value. o Example: If there are five slides for a quiz, then each slide is worth 20%. If a slide has eight points allocated across the questions, if you lose one point, the slide is worth (7/8) x 20 = 17.5% of the total quiz mark. o Feedback is through Ed, with answers and explanations provided within the assignment slide five days after the due date.

Assessment 2: Project 1 (Individual) Week 5, Sunday 22 March, 11:59 pm. 20% Identify, analyse and solve issues in code, explaining your rationale. Ed platform CLO1: Develop a toolkit of essential financial market tools applicable to various real-world scenarios. CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data. Task Description In this task, you will act as a code reviewer for Python modules that simulate financial data processing tools. These files may include bugs or incomplete logic. Your job is to: • Diagnose errors, • Fix only what’s broken, and • Explain your reasoning clearly, using data to justify each correction. This task reflects real-world technical debugging processes used in finance teams, illustrating how AI and collaborative coding work in financial settings. The project develops your problem-solving and communication skills. What you Need to Do and Deliverables For each task file (e.g., task_x.py), you must submit the following on Ed : 1. Activity diagram a. A diagram showing how the functions in the module connect. b. Submit as an image or PDF. 2. Corrected Python file (.py) a. Only fix code that is not working properly. b. Leave working code unchanged . 3. Explanation and examples a. Describe every modification to the supplied Python code. b. Use examples from the supplied data to explain why every correction was necessary. What You’re Not Allowed to Do (The constraints) • Don’t modify functions that work as expected. • Don’t modify the “main()” functions in any module. • Don’t change function names, signatures, or docstrings.

• Don’t import new libraries. • Don’t add new functions. • Don’t change the config.py module. AI Use – What’s Allowed ? You may use Generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Bing Chat) to support your work , but you must: • Explain how and why you used it in your Explanation (e.g., to debug, clarify logic). • Acknowledge the tool used and the prompt or output (if applicable). • Critically evaluate any AI-generated content. Do not copy without understanding. Do not use AI to write your final code directly. Undisclosed AI use is misconduct and may result in penalties, including a failing grade or exclusion. For guidance on ethical use and referencing AI, see: UNSW AI in Teaching Guidelines Grading and Feedback Week 7 classes will include a detailed discussion of the project. We will review each function, identifying those that worked as intended and those with bugs. Each bug will be corrected. That is, stu
